thread.title: Thousands of people in France are calling for a new law against sexist violence

author: time news
title: Thousands of people in France are calling for a new law against sexist violence
text:
Thousands of people demonstrated on Saturday in several ‚Å£French cities to demand a new law‚Å£ against sexist‚Å§ violence including the notion of‚Äå consent‚Äç brought to the ‚Å£fore ‚Äåby the case of Gis√®le‚Äå Pelicot, ‚Äåraped in an unconscious state by at least 51 defendants, including her ex-husband.
As this high-profile‚Äã trial enters‚Å¢ its final phase ‚Äì the final conclusions are expected next week before the‚Äå December ‚Äå20 ruling ‚Äì more than‚Å§ 400 associations have brought‚Äç forward the‚Äã deadlines by two days. World Day for the Elimination ‚Äåof Violence against Women and they called protests‚Äç in numerous French cities.
The one in Paris was ‚Äçthe busiest. In‚Äç it‚Å§ they denounced him ‚Å£ in France there is a rape every six minutes; that only 0.5% of‚Äç them lead to a conviction; and that ‚Äãevery three ‚Äãdays there is a femicide.
Furthermore, the associations complained‚Å¢ about the lack of financial ‚Äåmeans to stop sexist violence in France and called for a organic law inspired by that of Spain, which‚Äå has, for example, ‚Äãcourts specializing in violence against women. The notion of sexual consent was ‚Å¢also one of the ‚Äãflags of the‚Äå marches.
Activist Sophie Truchot, one of the founders of ‚ÄúWhy ‚Å¢feminism‚Äù (why feminism?‚Äô), assured the BFMTV channel‚Äç that the trial of‚Äã the 51‚Äå accused of raping Gis√®le Pelicot showed ‚Å§the need to legally frame‚Äç consent.
‚ÄúOne of the‚Äã pretexts used by the defendants was to say that they didn‚Äôt know whether ‚Å£Ms. Pelicot had consented. It‚Äôs a fairly weak ‚Å£argument, ‚Äãgiven‚Äå the ‚Äãevidence, but it shows ‚Å¢how‚Äç important it is to introduce it ‚Äãinto the law,‚Äù Truchot said.
A parliamentary mission from the French‚Å£ National Assembly will work until the ‚Å£end of the year ‚Äçto introduce consent into the criminal definition‚Å§ of rape which, until now, includes‚Äç any act ‚Äåof ‚Äúviolence, pressure, threat or surprise‚Äù without explicitly ‚Äçmentioning consent.
However, a change‚Äã in the current legal framework‚Äç divides jurists, feminist associations, parliamentarians and ‚Äçjudges. French ‚ÄåJustice Minister Didier ‚Å£Migaud said he was in favor of introducing consent.
This concept has become the ‚Å¢order of the day since ‚ÄãGis√®le Pelicot‚Äôs rape‚Å£ trial ‚Äåbegan last September, the latest hearings of which took place in Avignon (south of‚Å¢ France). The facts in question ‚Å£occurred between ‚Äå2011 and 2020 period in which this seventy-year-old was drugged with massive doses of anxiolytics administered by ‚Å£her then husband, Dominique, without her knowledge and which left her‚Äå in a state of unconsciousness that prevented her ‚Äçfrom remembering what ‚Äçhappened.
From the city of Maz√°n where‚Äå they lived, her husband then made ‚Å§contact with ‚Å§other men on an online‚Äã platform dedicated to sexual encounters and invited‚Å§ them to his home to rape‚Äå Gis√®le.‚Å¢ Dominique Pelicot‚Äç recorded and photographed these rapes,‚Å¢ documents which became the ‚Å¢main pieces of evidence for the prosecution.
During the almost three months of the trial, which Gis√®le expressly‚Äç asked to be held publicly and to which 165 media were‚Å¢ accredited, there were emotional statements from the three Pelicot ‚Å§children‚Äã and the victim herself,‚Äå who assured this week‚Äç that ‚Äúthe Scar will not stay closed.‚Äù ‚Å¢‚ÄúI lost ten years of‚Å¢ my life that I will ‚Å£never get back,‚Äù emphasized Gis√®le, who is currently 72 years old.
How can public awareness campaigns impact the success of new legislation on consent in France? Interview Between Time.news Editor ‚Å¢and ‚ÄãExpert on Sexist Violence‚Å¢ Legislation in ‚ÄãFrance
Time.news Editor (TNE): Welcome to our discussion today. We‚Äôre ‚Äãjoined by Dr. Emily Roche, a legal‚Å£ expert ‚Å¢in gender-based violence law and a leading advocate for reform in France. Thank you ‚Äãfor being here, Dr. Roche.
Dr. Emily Roche (ER): Thank you for ‚Å£having me. It‚Äôs a crucial time to discuss these issues.
TNE: ‚Äå Absolutely. Over the weekend, thousands gathered across France demanding stronger laws against sexist violence,‚Äå particularly in light of the high-profile case involving Gis√®le ‚ÄåPelicot. What can you tell ‚Äåus about the‚Äç significance of this case?
ER: The Gis√®le Pelicot case is a harrowing example of how ‚Äãdeeply ingrained‚Å£ issues surrounding sexual violence are‚Å§ in‚Å§ our society. The fact that 51 individuals were implicated in her assault underscores a systemic problem ‚Å£not just in legal enforcement but also in societal perceptions of consent. This case is galvanizing public support for ‚Äãreforms that emphasize the‚Äå importance of consent in legal definitions.
TNE: The protest highlighted alarming statistics: a rape every six minutes and only 0.5% leading‚Å§ to a conviction. How ‚Äçdoes France‚Äôs current legal framework contribute to these statistics?
ER: ‚Å¢The ‚Äçcurrent framework is inadequate‚Äå in several ways. The legal definition of rape in‚Å¢ France lacks a clear articulation of consent. Presently, it focuses on violence and pressure, which allows many perpetrators to exploit ambiguities. This system‚Äå not only reflects but also‚Äç perpetuates a culture where victims often feel disempowered and unheard.
TNE: There have been calls for legislation similar to that of Spain, which includes specialized ‚Äçcourts for violence against women. How could such measures transform the landscape in France?
ER: ‚ÄçImplementing a specialized‚Äã legal framework‚Å£ like that of Spain could dramatically improve how cases of gender-based violence are handled. Specialized courts would help‚Äç ensure that ‚Å¢cases are assessed‚Å§ by judges who ‚Å£understand the complexities of‚Äç such issues. Moreover, they could lead‚Äã to more informed judgments and a more‚Äå supportive atmosphere for victims seeking justice.
TNE: Activist Sophie Truchot mentioned that the trial revealed the urgent need to legally define consent. What challenges do you foresee in introducing this ‚Å¢definition into French law?
ER: One of the main challenges will be overcoming deeply entrenched‚Äã societal attitudes. There are those who resist changing existing definitions of rape, fearing it‚Äã may complicate prosecutions. Yet, the‚Å¢ context of the Pelicot trial demonstrates that clarity on consent could actually benefit legal ‚Äåprocesses. The argument of ‚Äúunclear consent‚Äù allowed the defendants‚Äç to evade accountability. We need to change this narrative.
TNE: The parliamentary‚Å£ mission aims to introduce a consent-focused definition by the end of the year. What steps do you believe need to be ‚Äåtaken to ensure this mission is successful?
ER: First, there needs to‚Å¢ be a broad coalition of support. This involves not just legislators but also civil‚Å¢ society, activists, and legal professionals coming together to advocate for this ‚Äåchange. Public awareness campaigns ‚Å£can also educate people about the importance of consent. Lastly, we must ensure that any new legislation is accompanied‚Äå by adequate funding for training law enforcement and judicial personnel so they can effectively adapt to‚Å§ these changes.
TNE: With such pressing issues at‚Äç hand,‚Äã what‚Å£ role do you see the public playing in ‚Äçthe push for legislative reform?
ER: The public‚Äôs role ‚Å£is‚Å§ pivotal. The protests over the weekend demonstrate that ‚Å§there is a significant ‚Äãdemand for change. Public pressure‚Äã can force the government to ‚Å§act and prioritize these issues. As activists continue ‚Äåto raise their voices and share statistics that highlight the severity of the crisis, we may see actual legislative momentum.
TNE: Thank you, Dr. Roche, ‚Äãfor sharing your‚Äã insights. It‚Äôs clear that this is ‚Å¢a moment of critical importance for the future of gender-based violence legislation in France.
ER: Thank‚Äç you for bringing attention to this vital issue. Together, we can strive to create a legal system that truly protects and respects victims.
